competitors.label: Semantics
competitors.selectionReason: This is the closest canonical MDN page for semantics in HTML and why choosing meaningful elements changes how content is interpreted.
theirStrengths: MDN is concise and authoritative on what semantics means and why some elements are meaningful while others are generic.
theirGaps: The glossary treatment is shorter and less concrete on landmarks, debugging, and the practical failure mode of div soup.

realWorldUseCases: help screen readers navigate, help search engines understand structure, and make large layouts easier to debug.
actionableExamples: screen-reader landmarks disappear and DevTools gives you less structure to debug.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains semantics clearly, but our answer now ties semantics to landmarks, search structure, and debugging in one production-focused frame.
actionableExamples: The glossary is still lighter on concrete before-and-after examples than our div-soup comparison.
followUpConfusion: MDN still makes the semantic-versus-nonsemantic distinction clear, so the follow-up criterion remains close.
Keep the section-versus-article note visible so the answer stays strong on likely follow-up questions.

competitors.winReason: MDN is authoritative on the definition, but our page is now more useful as an interview answer because it turns semantics into concrete landmarks, div-soup debugging, and a visible before-and-after example.

competitors.label: Semantic HTML
competitors.selectionReason: web.dev has a direct semantic HTML article that explains landmarks, document meaning, and the accessibility tree with clear before-and-after examples.
theirStrengths: It is very strong on semantic versus non-semantic examples, landmarks, and how semantic HTML affects the accessibility object model.
theirGaps: It is broader and less compressed into a short trivia answer.

realWorldUseCases: help screen readers navigate, help search engines understand structure, and make large layouts easier to debug.
followUpConfusion: a themed part of the page that usually has its own heading.

realWorldUseCases: web.dev connects semantics to the accessibility tree well, but our answer now packages the landmark, SEO, and debugging payoffs more directly for a real-world interview response.
actionableExamples: web.dev still has very strong examples, so the actionable-example criterion stays tied rather than becoming a full sweep.
followUpConfusion: Our page now handles the likely follow-up more directly with explicit section, article, and div decision rules.
